comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG and thank you for another Magic Monday, right on time as usual. I have a couple of questions about your personal magical practice and writing habits:

1. What is your go-to divination method these days (other than astrology)?

2. Do you prefer to jump headfirst into new writing projects or do you do lots of methodical research and preparation beforehand? How do you know when you've done enough research and it's time to start writing?

3. Are you involved in the design of your book covers (specifically, I have in mind the Occult Book, the Conspiracy book, and the Dolmen Arch), or is it more of a publisher responsibility?

4. Back when you were more involved with occult groups and Masonic lodges, what was the preferred means of communication between the members? How did you recruit new members? I assume you weren’t using social media back then either?

5. How many hours in a given day would you estimate that you spend in front of a computer screen? If this is a high number, do you find this problematic at all? Nicholas Carr wrote a book called The Shallows in which he argues that the Internet exerts a negative effect on memory and cognition. Do you agree with this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Right now it's my Sacred Geometry Oracle, which will be out in a new edition soon, and which is also central to a new system of occult training I'm developing.

2) However much research I do in advance, I end up having to do more as the writing process turns up new questions and ideas. Thus I usually start writing as soon as I have a good basic idea of how I want to begin the book, and research and write in parallel. (This is as true of novels as it is of nonfiction books, btw.)

3) It's the publisher's job. I'm very pleased that a few publishers of mine actively solicit my input into the cover art.

4) Masonic lodges are one thing and occult lodges are quite another. Most Masonic lodges use the internet all the time. Magical lodges work best in silence, and usually recruit by personal recommendation.

5) There's a difference between sitting in front of a computer screen and using the internet. My writing computer is not connected to the internet; my wife and I have a dedicated internet computer on which we keep no files of value, to avoid certain obvious problems, and outside of Mondays I usually spend 1-2 hours online per day. Yes, it's a dumbing-down medium, but some parts of it are much stupider than others; I use it almost exclusively to answer email, manage my blog and this journal, and find and download old and otherwise unavailable occult texts.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 5) Do you think the dumbing down online is inherent to the internet? Do you think it could be turned into a more positive medium?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure, but you'd be fighting the entire corporate establishment. Dumb people are more likely to buy shoddy products, after all...

comments.title: Evolution...
comments.content:
You’ve said a couple things recently, though I can’t remember where, that suggested you believe beings are reincarnated “up the ladder” (my phrase) towards humanity and consciousness. Am I remembering wrong?

If true, this seems a weirdly chauvinistic belief in favor of consciousness by those who happen to have it. My dog doesn’t seem any less advanced/evolved than me, and trees don’t seem any less advanced/evolved than either of us. In fact, there’s a tree in a graveyard near my house I’m convinced is an ascended master! :)

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
You're certainly free to disagree with this bit of classic occult philosophy if you like. It's not accurate, though, to equate humanity and consciousness. All things are conscious to one degree or another, and your dog is fully capable of a rich experiential and emotional life. What differentiates you from your dog is the first feeble stirrings of activity on what occultists call the mental plane, which is the plane of abstract meaning; you and I can sometimes, to a very limited extent, think in abstract terms, while dogs think wholly in concrete terms -- the way we do 90% or more of the time.

As any Greek pagan could have told you, btw, it's not the tree who's the ascended master, it's the guardian spirit of the tree -- the dryad, in Greek terms. You and your dog also have guardian spirits, but for complex reasons it's somewhat easier for human beings to experience the guardian spirits of plants and of "inanimate" things like rivers and mountains. The tree, like you, me, your dog, and the planet earth herself, are all in the process of evolution, although at different stages -- and there are beings as far beyond you and me, in terms of intelligence and complexity, as we are beyond pond scum. That's what occult philosophy teaches, at any rate.

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content: Thanks for your reply. Is a guardian spirit distinct from the “etheric body” which you mentioned in a reply to me a few weeks ago?

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. The levels of being go roughly like this:

Spiritual Planes
Mental Plane
Astral Plane
Etheric Plane
Material Plane

So you have a material body, which is meat; an etheric body, which is the body of life force (prana, qi, voor, etc.); you have an astral body, which is the body of concrete consciousness (the level on which you do most of your thinking and your dog does all of its thinking); and a mental sheath, the first very rough draft of the mental body you'll evolve over many lives. Above that are the spiritual planes. Guardian spirits, as the name suggests, belong to the spiritual planes, though they can act downward into the planes below.

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
So, what IS a guardian spirit? Is is distinct from the higher self? Is it analogous to the Holy Guardian Angel from Abramelin?
If so, why do we have guardians? It's just hard to accept because it smacks of the typical mentality of "I'm so special that the universe has given me extra special helpers."

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
1) A guardian spirit is a being of a more advanced evolution, whose further development is benefited by helping one or more beings of a later evolution. Yes, it's distinct from your higher self; yes, your HGA is such a being.

2) You're not special. You have a guardian spirit; so does your dog; so does the tree in the graveyard; so do the dust mites that live in your pubic hair. The universe is just like that.

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content: 2) I suspect that the spirit who pulled pubic mite duty is possibly working off some karma. :)

replies.content: It depends. From a certain point of view it could be rather entertaining. ;-)

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
Almost a year ago a friend did a Tarot reading for me which quickly turned into a bit more than just a reading and said “they were screaming in frustration and were basically dragging me and questioning why wouldn’t I just move” out of a strong period of my life which is still lingering and close to its end, so it feels. Then two days ago I did a tarot reading regarding where should I take my spirituality to, since what I have been noticing has basically made it clear that there is much, much more to Life than what I could possibly imagine and I have spun into a frenzy of looking for something that I can pursue for at least a long period of time if not the rest of my life in order to give it the respect and dedication such things deserve. I didn’t even put so much thought and research to choosing what to study in College! The reading was very fuzzy and when I was about to pick up the cards I noticed a train of thought, something like a thread in my mind and when I grabbed it I just said out loud very quickly: “Take your mind to a different place than where it is right now and then come to me”. Said and done the next day, with a much more calm mind I came to the reading and started looking for the same mental threads and without much thought just writing the first things that came to my mind. It was awesome, creepy and certainly made sense with what my friend was telling me a year ago and even though it was basically the same message than what he told me back then I think I am much more prepared to make something out of it. What you said to me last Monday helped me a lot! I think I have been holding back in fear of being wrong, instead of just letting it come out because I am treating it like an infallible source of truth rather than advise that is still subject to my judgement. Where I am trying to get at is:

1)How can I start making a distinction between what comes from, say, journaling with some emotions and divination? Both seem to be related to finding and tuning into a thread that you just follow similar to when inspiration comes. Or more technically how can I make a distinction between tuning with my psychological patterns and letting them speak and tuning with the Astral Light and what my guardian spirit has to say?

2) I’ve been pondering a lot into the magical implications of everyday life and something that has got me into trouble is for example thinking about other people. I feel more cautious about what I think but have also become afraid of expressing for example anger in my mind. Is there a magical effect about having such feelings in ones mind while thinking about others?

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
1) That sort of distinction is something you have to learn to do; it takes time and practice. Fortunately both sources give useful feedback.

2) There can be. If you focus your anger on another person it can set magical processes in motion that can harm them, and generate blowback for you -- this is especially true if you fantasize about hurting them, or what have you. Acknowledge the anger, accept that this is where your mind is at right now, and then turn your attention to something else. Again, it takes practice!

Has anyone made estimates of what percentages of people are at these different levels of evolution? Highly evolved people don't seem to be running the show, they aren't most politicians, or closed-minded professional cadres.

You suggested a while ago, that there are currently a lot of souls taking their first turn at being human, and that this could explain the amount of angry, doctrinaire people influencing culture.

Have there been particular times and places where there was a higher percentage of evolved people or where they had more power in society?

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
1) It varies constantly from time to time, since souls who have completed their work on the human level are passing beyond it, while new souls are coming into the human level, and both of these happen irregularly, depending on the fine details of who's achieved what in which life. I suspect that under most circumstances it's kind of a bell-shaped curve, with most people somewhere in the middle and only a few on either end.

2) The people I've met who I'd guess are brand new at the human level aren't particularly doctrinaire, though they do repeat a lot of sound bites, and they're angry only when they've been told to be angry. They absorb what they're told by the media and repeat it back word for word, but there's no real commitment or understanding, just the human equivalent of one sheep bleating to another.

3) Yes. Look at the great ages of human culture, such as classical Greece or the European Renaissance, and notice how many well-known figures in those times had the kind of remarkable talents that you only get by lifetimes of work. Those periods are always brief, because the souls who make them what they are move on past the human level, but a great deal of our species' greatest creations come into being in such times.

replies.title: Re: Evolution...
replies.content:
This is true. Which is why it's only very advanced persons whom might choose to do all the practices that let them stay in the material realm when their Consciousness has gained enough "velocity" (in a manner of speaking) to ascend. According to Sadhguru for 99% of people the moment of enlightenment is the same moment of their death on the physical plane because the physical layer can't hold them anymore.

There's also one very interesting twist according to Yogic lore. If one can stay fully, objectively conscious during the final 40 seconds of the whole death process (first death, second death, etc) then That Which is Not - the Enlightened Stillness - finishes all of your karma completely. For that person who has stayed objectively conscious throughout the whole process - all 16 overarching aspects of Mind stay fully awake and functioning - there will be no more human rebirths. That person has ascended for good. That's why Tibetan Buddhists place so much emphasis on the Bardo and practice for it. Their goal is to take advantage of that 40 second window of opportunity for complete karma burn. As the saying goes, "Samsara is Nirvana, when rightly seen."

3. Are all the different occult systems, Eastern, Western and otherwise, manifestations of the same system of knowledge with a different coat of paint, or do they refer to fundamentally different realities?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Any given set of placements has to be understood in the context of the rest of the chart, and no, I'm not up to spending an hour or so delineating your chart to make sense of it! As for whether or not your stars favor occultism, remember the old Rosicrucian motto: the wise govern their stars, fools are governed by them. If it's your will to pursue occultism, do it.

2) Your higher self is your real self; it's who you actually are. Your personality is a temporary mask worn by the higher self as it descends into the world of experience. Thus the full potential of your personality is your higher self, and it's not the same for any two beings in all the cosmos. Jung knew what he was talking about.

3) Roughly halfway between those two extremes. All occult systems exist within the same universe and make use of some subset of the available options, so there are definite parallels between systems, but the differences aren't just skin deep, and the endpoints are not all the same by any means.

comments.title: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

An interesting thing happened yesterday. I have a small Cthulhu statue that sits on the mantle of a disused fireplace. The chimney sometimes leaks when it rains and water drips through the ceiling. After a heavy downpour yesterday I found that the water was dripping exactly on the top of the Cthulhu statue. It missed everything around it and even the picture on the wall was dry. Only the Cthulhu statue was wet and sitting in its own little puddle. Would there be any significance to this? How do you think I should interpret this and what should I do next, if anything?

replies.title: Re: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
replies.content:
A traditional polytheist would take that as a signal that Great Cthulhu is interested in interacting with you. If you want to take up the offer, there are various ways to do it -- the simplest and safest would be the twice-daily meditative ritual I put into my novel The Weird of Hali: Kingsport.

(Normally I don't recommend trying to work with fictional deities, but people have been invoking the deities of the Cthulhu mythos and getting results since Kenneth Grant's time. For that matter, while I was working on my Mythos novels, it was very clear to me that I was interacting with something that was far from fictional. Your mileage may vary...)

replies.title: Re: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
replies.content: I get that feeling from READING Lovecraftiana, as well as from some of Stephen King’s earlier work.  Also from others here and there.  When I finally got to read Joseph Payne Brennan’s “Nine Horrors and a Dream,”  all the stories were entertaining horror fiction, EXCEPT “Canavan’s Back Yard.”  That one had the hard cold ring of terrible truth.

replies.title: Re: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
replies.content: I get it from Lovecraft, not from most of the others. I don't know if you've looked at my very odd contributions to the genre, but they unfold from the simple fact that I don't find the Mythos deities frightening. The only Lovecraft story I find actually scary is "The Color out of Space." The others? To me they're fantasy, not horror; the idea that the universe doesn't notice or care about our existence strikes me not merely as common sense but as actually rather comforting. Of course I also think that octopi are cute. Thus in my Mythos novels, the Great Old Ones are the old gods of nature, those sinister and multiethnic cultists are occultists who've been vilified in the usual way with the usual blood libels, and the bad guys are basically Delta Green on bad steroids with an extra dose of lust for power and a fanatic belief that human beings ought to run the cosmos. Yes, the main character's love interest in the first volume has tentacles in place of legs, and yes, I got that from the movie Dagon. ;-)

replies.title: Re: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
replies.content: Thank you, I will give this a try. Now to find potpourri during the lockdown...

replies.title: Re: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
replies.content: Rosemary leaves would be a good start -- they're widely used for sea magic. A blend of rosemary leaves and spices with a bitter element, such as cloves, might be a good option to start with; you can add new ingredients and explore essential oils when you have the chance.

replies.title: Re: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
replies.content: Wouldn't these observations then point to the probability that Lovecraft himself might have been interacting with real entities?

replies.title: Re: Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Cthulhu R'lyeh wgah'nagl fhtagn
replies.content: We know he did in one sense. When he was a boy, he was crazy about Greek mythology, and took it to the extent of setting up an altar in his backyard and pouring libations to the gods and goddesses of Olympus. I don't think it's at all accidental that his fictive mythology resonates so closely with actual powers -- or that there are definite similarities between the way that the Greek gods in Homer relate to human beings, and the way that the Great Old Ones relate to their worshipers in Lovecraft's stories!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) The archetype of the successful male in contemporary Western culture generally involves material wealth shown off through conspicuous consumption, high social status, handsome looks (extra points for a sculpted, muscular physique), and a string of lovers tumbling into his bed. Are such portrayals an image created by marketers and entertainment moguls to erode men’s self-esteem and promote products, or is it something that corresponds to a genuine reality? And do you have any advice for those of us who don’t conform to it, particularly in the sense that it's become almost an expectation in the dating market?

2) In Japan, there is a sociological phenomenon called hikikomori, where upwards of a million people have withdrawn completely from society, never leaving their homes (and often, their bedrooms) for years on end. 75-90% of hikikomori are estimated to be men, but women may be underrepresented in the statistics for various reasons. They’re supported by their parents or relatives into their 40's and 50’s, although a minority make an independent living through remote work. The withdrawal of the hikikomori is usually caused by factors such as academic pressure, exam failure, bullying, workplace stress, or job loss. Do you have any thoughts on this condition, and what might be going on in the psyches of the hikikomori?

3) Why do you think the divorce rates are so high in Western countries? Depending on how it’s measured and what country is being considered, estimates range from anywhere between 30-60%. Presumably, most people don’t get married with the expectation that they’ll get divorced someday, and yet it seems to happen with unfailing regularity, on average, 7 years after the vows are exchanged. Is this sort of breakdown an inevitable feature of human relationships? Clearly, making divorce impossible led to miseries of its own, but do we have to accept such a high rate of failure for the minority(?) of successful relationships out there, considering the fallout that often accompanies divorces?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's not a genuine reality -- far from it. As Ioan Couliano pointed out many years ago in Eros and Magic in the Renaissance, modern advertising is a corrupt and malevolent form of magic. The image you've described is pushed on you by the media to keep you feeling weak and inferior, just as a comparable image (I'm sure you can describe that just as clearly) is pushed on women with the same goal in mind. The result is that 90% of guys chase blindly after the 10% or so of women who more or less correspond to the image, and 90% of women chase just as blindly after the 10% or so of men who likewise more or less correspond to their image.

As for what to do about it, depends on whether you're willing to extract yourself from both sides of the equation. I was fortunate enough to catch on to this in my teen years, and learn to appreciate a wider range of feminine charms; as a result, I was only rarely and briefly without a girlfriend from the time I turned sixteen until I got married six years later. (Yes, we're still married; our 36th anniversary is coming up in July.)

2) I've read about it but I don't claim to understand it. I have Aspergers syndrome, and asking an Aspie about what's going on in someone else's psyche is not necessarily going to get you far. ;-)

3) No, that sort of breakdown isn't inevitable at all. (As my case demonstrates.) Our culture raises barriers in the way of successful long-term relationships, but they can be overcome so long as both people involved are willing to work at it. The key is being able to step outside a purely self-centered perspective and see what your actions and words look like from the other person's perspective, If both partners cultivate that, there's a very good chance the marriage will last. Of course here again, that's something the marketers and the media do their best to prevent, since miserable people buy more products!

replies.content:
My take on the high divorce rate in Western Countries complements our host's take on it.

Most folk, so it seems to me, expect that all the work of making a marriage is over and done with the moment both parties exchange their vows: after that comes the rather different, alien work of making a married life (getting such things as a house and yard, children, social position, etc. ad nauseum).

No; exchanging your vows is when the real work of making a marriage finally begins, and that work never ever stops until the day you die. And it's work that stands completely apart from the consumer economy, from the all-too-human quest for status and/or power, etc. This work cannot be monetized; it cannot become a part of any consumer economy; there is no possible way to do that. It's more like the very hard, unrelenting work of a life-long spiritual path.

My wife and I have both been doing this marriage work for nigh on 53 years now, consciously and deliberately, and we plan to keep doing it for the rest of our lives. It's really paid off for both of us. We can honestly say that we're each other's best friend. There are also other real benefits that come with long, long, long marriages, thoughtfully built and maintained.

One of the more surprising things we discovered is that a long-married eventually couple develop, in additon to their own individual Selves, a shared Self as well. The syzygy--the "yoke"--you have voluntarily undertaken to share as you work together will develop a consciousness and awareness of its own, which turns out to be a different thing from each partner's own personal consciousness and awareness. It's fun to have this, and it's also useful.

[I should mention here that my own family's esoteric theories posit that each human being consists of many interdependent Selves, which naturally are created in the course of living one's life. For us, the new-born baby has no Self at all, but must construct the various Selves that it will need to live its life as it grows and matures and ripens and dies. (Jung, I think, got it quite backwards with his idea of an undivided, "individuated," human being as a desirable goal.) -- This theory of ours has its roots, so far as I can tell, in the sort of experiences and experiments that led people like Frederic W. H. Myers to talk about "subliminal" selves that people have quite apart from their "liminal" selves, through which they present themselves to the world. (And before Myers, there were the experiments of Mesmer. So it's a very old-fashioned theory, predating and--it seems to me--very much at odds with the sort of cosmology that Madame Blavatsky (and eventually, Dion Fortune) talk about in their writings.]

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: FWIW, at least in the US, the divorce rate for first marriages is not especially high - you get the "half of all marriages" statistics from a minority of people who divorce and remarry several times - and has been declining for some time. The highest divorce rates have been the from generations where marriage was highly normative and expected, and then no-fault divorce came in and gave them more options. Younger generations have divorced less in part because if they get married, they're more likely to really intend to, and also more likely to do it a little later in life, when decisions are less impulsive.

replies.title: Re: Banishing.
replies.content: A proper banishing ritual, such as the LBRP or the Sphere of Protection, is designed to balance the elemental forces in your aura and your surroundings under the direction and guidance of Spirit. Any entity or energy that can abide the presence of Spirit and a condition of elemental balance will be perfectly able to come and go in a banished space; the entities and energies that can't tolerate Spirit and can't maintain themselves in a balanced condition are driven off. Thus anything you actually need to have around you -- for example, unpleasant experiences that you need to balance your karma -- will stick around before, during, and after a banishing ritual; it's the stuff you don't need -- noxious spirits, nasty magic, other people's thoughts and feelings, etc. -- that gets swept away.

Dion Fortune came to her Christian faith only as an adult: she says herself that she was raised as a Christian Scientist. So maybe the conflict between Christian and Pagan forces wasn't as strong in her as it would be in a baptized (and confirmed) Christian.

Christian Science has nothing even remotely resembling the traditional Christian sacraments: no baptism, no confirmation, no funerals ... not even any such thing as church marriages. (Sure, Christian Scientists marry all the time, but their only option is a civil marriage. Mary Baker Eddy taught that parents could conceive their children without any such thing as physical sex, if only they had developed their Christian Science sufficiently.) There is such a thing as Christian Science communion, which happens four times a year, but it makes no use of bread or wine or anything else that can be eaten or drunk, and it entails no vows or promises, explicit or implicit. So she wouldn't have been either baptized or confirmed as a child.

comments.content:
Last week, someone touched briefly on discernment, picking out real divine messages from the tangle of your own thoughts. I'd had a relationship with a particular god that I called a patronage but have recently come to realize was actually not. I've known it for a while in the back of my mind, but I've since had it confirmed by multiple outside divinations - either it's been over for a long time, or it never actually was outside of a couple very powerful experiences in the very beginning.

My background is in NeoPaganism though I'm no longer involved there. I've been realizing they don't do a good job with discernment, and may have done much to set up a mistake like this. I think it was on your other blog, a while ago you described NeoPaganism as focused more on emotional states than spiritual realities with a lot of pop culture notions getting in the way, I think you're absolutely right about that. A lot of what gets promoted as the way humans and gods interact is little more than bad fantasy fiction tropes, that get more extreme if you're out in the, uh, kookier end of things, which I was for a time, wading neck deep in Chosen Ones. I remember a culture that actively discouraged critical thought (which got a lot worse as the current political trends began creeping in more), blind acceptance of anything that came along, anything someone told you, whether they're a reincarnation of a ten year old anime character or worshipping demons, it's all good and who are you to question them even in the privacy of your own thoughts? If you can't question anything, how do you question your own experiences, or keep those other experiences from influencing your own? There was no understanding (I almost want to say it seems like a deliberate misunderstanding) of group minds, collective narratives and the affect they can have in shaping or outright creating your own thoughts (that latter was something I experienced for a time, thankfully brief but very confusing, nothing I'd learned from the NeoPagan corners I was in gave me any context for what had happened, I had to piece it together on my own).

Even though I knew almost from the beginning (and I know that, because I was more active on the internet then and wrote about it a lot) that something was wrong, I was actively discouraged from considering this option, that it was a mistake, as being the root of the problem. No matter how long things didn't work, whatever time or effort I made, just keep going with it, forever if need be, even if it never works again, just do don't ask questions. The possibility of being wrong, that your interpretation of spiritual experiences was incorrect and a lot less glamorous than you may have first thought, doesn't seem to be something people want to grapple with. Can't blame them, it hasn't been much fun.

I'm still struggling to figure out how this happened, how I lost control of this, how it got this bad. I have no doubt a lot of the mistakes made were all my own (in the very beginning, when I knew no better, it's no wonder), but I also think group dynamics, the particular culture in NeoPaganism (the parts I was involved in), the expectations they implant in you, instruction not given, questions never asked, played a big part in this, too.

My question for you:

1. How do you develop good discernment, separating your thoughts from the gods (or someone else's)? How do you overcome a background like this, separating out the bad instruction and unrealistic expectations so you can move forward, and hopefully not into a second blunder of the same sort? I have no wish to give up, on the gods or the path of mysticism I had as my goal, but what can I do to try and prevent something like this from happening again?

2. As I'm not the Chosen One, what could I do to find/attract a patron deity - for the more intense, devotional, mystical kind of connection? For when I am ready to go there again.

I also just want to throw out a quick thanks to Violet. Her comments here about her relationship with Aphrodite in particular have been a help in this mess, in giving another perspective and sorting a few ideas out in my head. I've really appreciated reading them.

replies.title: discernment
replies.content:
What JMG says in comment 1) is fundamental.

I'd like to add my own two cents, writing from a perspective of about forty-five years of participation in coven-centered wiccanate witchcraft, from the more neopagan (feminist Dianic witchcraft) to the more systematic and rule-bound (Gardnerian Wicca), and including a regional mystery tradition that is sui generis.

At some point you might feel a need to dip your toes into group work again, or want to participate in a community in a less intensive way than by joining a group. You will probably be worried about making the same mistakes again, or getting into some other kind of trouble. If you have developed discernment (about people, groups, ideas, goals and entities), you will be much quicker at sizing up people and the opportunities that may be presented to you. You are no longer naive. You are developing your will, your self knowledge and your psychic abilities. You are probably getting better already at noticing when you are being led in a particular direction. You are getting the tools to figure out who or what is doing the leading.

If the time comes when you want to return to group work, you might get the best benefit from one of two types. One would be a formal hierarchical group with a set cirricultum and a visible boundary between what is part of the system and what is not. JMG has written books presenting several systems of that type. If you join a group that claims to be working a system, whether it's a form of Druidry or British Traditional Wica or some other thing, keep an eye out for whether their behavior matches their pretensions, and how people advance to positions of authority.

Another possibility is that you will find your passion in a specific kind of magical work and then will need to find companions to compare notes with, watch each other's backs, and fill in the gaps in each other's knowledge and skills. To assemble a group of this kind, you will probably need to do some magic or prayer, certainly some divination, and make agreements with the people who turn up to the call, in order to set boundaries on what you do and what connections you form with each other. In that instance, you likely will be doing experimental work, and experimental work requires keeping records of results and doing peer review.

comments.title: Magic and addiction
comments.content:
Hello JMG!

I'd like to ask if there's any way in which magic can help deal with/overcome addiction and other self-destructive habits. Any guidance would be much appreciated, thanks!

replies.title: Re: Magic and addiction
replies.content:
Yes, but it's not a quick fix. The magical structure of addiction is that it's a division of the will: you want the object of the addiction, but you don't want to want it, and so you end up locked into a self-perpetuating cycle of trying to resist the addiction, failing, hating yourself for failing, trying again, and around you go.

To get out of the cycle you have to rebuild unity of will. The first step is to stop fighting and start managing. Stop pretending that you're in control of it, and stop telling other people or yourself that you'll stop, when you won't. Accept that you have the addiction and that you can't white-knuckle it into submission. See what you can do to manage it so that it does the least possible harm to other people. While you're doing that, spend some time journaling to see what emotional needs of yours the addiction meets -- very often there are all kinds of tangled emotional patterns tied up in it.

Once you've done this, look for other parts of your life -- completely unrelated to the addiction, and without any strong emotional loading at all -- where you can begin working on strengthening your will. Start deciding to do certain things for no reason at all, and then do them. For example, decide to notice every red thing you pass on the way to work. On your next day off -- assuming that the quarantine has been lifted! -- decide to go somewhere in town that you've never been, choose a time to be there, and be there on time. Choose a subject you know nothing about -- the history of Albania, say -- and learn about it. Make a game of it. Don't include anything about which you have strong feelings; keep it playful, calm, interesting.

Your daily magical practices are important here, too. Schedule them so that your addiction doesn't interfere with them, and vice versa. Keep on doing them. That's excellent will training all by itself.

As you continue in this way, you'll notice first of all that it becomes a lot easier for you to do things you choose to do, even when they're difficult. It also becomes easier for you to manage your addiction, and to make it even less of a burden on other people. Don't assault it directly; just keep on managing, willing, practicing, getting the rest of your life in order. Over time you'll find that the addiction matters less and less to you. You may suddenly realize that it's been a week since you last did whatever it is, and you hadn't noticed! That's what real recovery looks like.

replies.title: Re: Magic and addiction
replies.content:
a) So of course any attempt to go cold turkey is extremely counterproductive. Do you think it's a deliberate effort on someone's part to keep the idea cold turkey is the only way to go so widespread?

b) Here's an odd one: I've got an internet addiction. From what you've said, it would make sense for me to tackle it in an odd manner: ignoring the more destructive elements, and slowly whittling it down by targeting the stuff which I do so as to justify why I need it; I have a good sense of what I'm addicted to, and a lot of internet doesn't count. Does this make sense?

c) Is this part of why so many people relapse later? As much as they wanted the recovery, they still want whatever it is they were addicted to?

replies.title: Re: Magic and addiction
replies.content:
1) Exactly. Miserable people buy more products.

2) Yes, you could do it that way. The goal is to manage the addiction, not to justify it.

3) Exactly -- and they still have whatever emotional needs the addiction addressed.

replies.content: 1) Of course it's corporate influence.....

replies.content: Hmm. Is procrastination then the result of wanting to want something? You want to want it, but you don't actually, so the result is a self defeating pattern where you try to do something, then don't, because you don't actually want it, and then rinse and repeat and you have a procrastination problem....

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
Who were the mysterious "Sea Peoples"?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
They aren't mysterious at all. Toward the end of the bronze age, societies north of the eastern Mediterranean had a population boom driven by the spread of intensive farming methods from Mesopotamia. The problem was that those farming methods depended on fertilization by river silt, which you didn't get much of in Greece and points north. The result was a subsistence crisis -- severe soil erosion (so bad that it left recognizable layers in the sediments of the Aegean Sea), disastrous crop failures, the kind of conditions that lead warriors to take desperate risks. Furthermore, the northern peoples had good ships, and a lot of warriors from there had served as mercenaries in the eastern Mediterranean cultures, and understood the weaknesses of the high-tech (for the time) chariot armies of their former employers.

Robert Drews' very solid book The End of the Bronze Age chronicles what happened thereafter: tentative initial probings, followed by mass raids that sacked city after city along the eastern Mediterranean coast. The Hittite Empire ceased to exist, and so did dozens of other nations that had thrived for centuries. Finally the Sea Peoples launched a sreries of assaults on the biggest prize of all, Egypt -- but the Egyptians were ready for them. The Egyptian generals abandoned chariot warfare and raised a massive infantry army armed with spears and shields, met the Sea Peoples in a series of major battles, and crushed them. Surviving Sea Peoples fled to the far corners of the Mediterranean, and most of the Mediterranean world sank into a long and bitter dark age.

(If you're wondering why I had all this at my fingertips, I studied the late Bronze Age collapse extensively in the process of researching my novel Twilight's Last Gleaming -- it's one of the best examples on record of what I call asymmetric tactical shock, a process by which a less technologically advanced army monkeywrenches the more advanced technology of the enemy and renders the enemy helpless. An essay I wrote on the subject, "Asymmetric Tactical Shock; A First Reconnaissance" is included in my book of essays Beyond the Narratives.)

replies.content:

comments.content:
JMG,

I've been reflecting lately on how futile all of the attempts at changing the course of human civilization have been in the last 50 years. Here we are, accelerating towards disaster, our cultural institutions discredited and in freefall, with the current pandemic as a slap on the wrist, a warning sign of what's to come, and yet the only thought on most people's minds is how to kickstart the economy again and resume business as usual. Clearly, political ideology and activism has failed, technology has failed, capitalism has failed, the media has failed. There seems to be no off-ramp on the road we've set ourselves on, at least none that we could realistically take.

I think the fundamental problem is one of belief - our model of reality is flawed, and our behavior is derived from that. We believe in a closed and disempowering material universe, in a natural world that is blind and unintelligent, in reductionistic political ideologies, in the rationality of our economic system based on infinite growth on a finite planet, and in the sovereignty of the 'individual' to do as he pleases. We can't imagine acting otherwise, so we don't. So the problem is: how do you get people to change their minds? If only you could catalyze a change of perspective, and spread that throughout the population, everything else would flow from that, from the bottom up, because you can't bring about radical change through persuasion from the top down while maintaining the current set of assumptions; that's been tried and it doesn't work. A handful of exceptional individuals can go against the grain and change their behavior, but the mass of humanity will go along with the herd until the end, and if those few individuals become a threat they will be neutralized rather than be allowed to become examples. Some kind of global training course in magic would be desirable, but the political will is nonexistent, the ideological high priests would be opposed to it, and it would take far too long to have any effects.

So we need something else. Lecturing won't do it. PR campaigns aren't enough. Technology will be co-opted by special interests. Politics is irredeemably corrupt. The religions that have organizational clout are impotent. Institutional science has become a sock-puppet for corporatist technocratic interests. The only hope, as I see it, is a mass psychedelic experience. It's the only thing that will work quickly enough and on a large enough scale, and which isn't tied to mechanisms of central control. We need to organize something like a World LSD Day, a simultaneous 8-billion person trip that will bring us back to nature, that will dissolve the mass hallucination that we're trapped in. I'm aware of the echo of the 60's here, but I don't see any other viable options on the table. Under proper supervision, responsible use of psychedelics can have ego- and boundary-dissolving effects, which is what we most need. And I'm not talking about saving our current living arrangements, because that's clearly impossible, but about demobilizing in time to prevent us from slamming into a wall, and transitioning to something sustainable. But the change in perspective has to come first.

My guess, based on what's happening, is that after 2016 proved to them that TSW, some of the alt-right mages took up serious study of magical traditions more robust than chaos magic. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: From an occultist perspective, what was the purpose of mummification?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a long and rather bitter story.

A very long time ago, a specific set of practices was known by which a person could leave the cycle of reincarnation for a time in order to act as a guardian spirit for the living. That set of practices was certainly known from one end of Eurasia to the other; you can tell where it was practiced because it involved burial mounds -- big structures of stone covered by earth, in which the person went at the end of his or her life. (The stone and earth sheltered the etheric body from the rays of the sun.) The preservation of the physical body was essential to this, but that could be done -- as it was done in a few parts of the world until early modern times -- by a special diet alternating with longer and longer periods of fasting, which resulted in slow starvation and a body with basically nothing left that could rot.

Time passed, cultures changed, and the technique was abused. In some places the method was lost and the practice turned into a rite of human sacrifice. In others, it was taken over by chieftains and kings who wanted to use it to cheat death. That's the condition it had reached by the time of historic Egypt, and mummification was used in place of the old dietary method to preserve the body. Later on, the rites became mere formalities, and people got themselves mummified at death in the same spirit that corpses nowadays get pumped full of formaldehyde.

In a very few places, the old system survived more or less intact. In some others, a debased and predatory version of it survived, and gave rise to the legends of vampires. I discuss this last phase of the tradition in my book Monsters.

replies.title: recommended Bible versions
replies.content:
If you are interested in a scholarly Jewish perspective on the New Testament, have a look at The Jewish Annotated New Testament, edited by Amy-Jill Levine and Marc Zvi Brettler, Oxford University Press, 2011. Hardcover with bound signatures is $35.00 US; there might be a softcover version. HC ISBN is 978-0-19-529770-6.

I'm not mentioning this for the translation--it's the New Revised Standard Version, which is ubiquitous. Look at it for the front and back matter and the footnotes. The volume contains two introductory essays by the editors, "Bearing False Witness: Common Errors Made About Early Judaism" and "The New Testament between the Hebrew Bible (Tanakh) and Rabbinic Literature". Then 16 essays by various authors on History and Society (the one on "Jewish Miracle Workers in the Late Second Temple Period" is by Geza Vermes. Other topics include The Synagogue, Jewish Movements of the NT Period, Afterlife and Resurrection), 7 essays on Literature (Philo, Josephus, Dead Sea Scrolls, Midrash and Parable in the New Testament, etc.), and 5 essays on Jewish responses to the NT.

In the back are ten tables, including a timeline, a chronological table of rulers all over the area from 539 BCE to Hadrian, Greek and Hebrew weights and measures, etc.

Each book has an introduction of several pages, covering authorship, date of composition, style and content, structure and genre, and key themes. Some of the books also include historical maps. The notes to verses are extensive.

I think any layperson studying the NT from other than a strictly doctrinal perspective would find this edition to be a valuable reference work.
